text: A fifty-six-year-old obese man with bilateral inguinal hernia came to my observation with a strange symptom. After a mixturation the scrotal swelling decreased in size. To physical examination, the scrotum hernia seemed irreducible, so that the patient was subjected to an abdominal and pelvis TC.

The imaging reveals a large sliding inguinal hernia involving the urinary bladder, visible as a right scrotal systole cell. The surgical plan involves an extraperitoneal approach due to the bladder's extraperitoneal location.

The surgeon creates the preperitoneal space and inserts two 5mm trocars. The epigastric vessels are identified as anatomical landmarks, appearing detached from the abdominal wall, indicating a disruption of normal anatomy in this area.

The surgeon delicately dissects and liberates the hernia sac to reduce it, revealing the bladder, which is not covered by the peritoneal sac. The bladder is identified as extraperitoneal, and its reduction through the hernia orifice is challenging.

The surgeon works lateral to the epigastric vessels, identifying an external oblique hernia. After reducing the bladder, the anatomical structures become clearer, revealing funicular structures and another evident hernia. The sac of this second oblique external hernia is dissected and carefully isolated until it is reduced, with attention to identifying core structures to prevent recurrences.

The surgeon performs a final inspection of the surgical area, preparing for the placement of the prosthesis. Adhesions near the bladder and spermatic cord are carefully sectioned to ensure clear access and positioning.
A macroporous partially absorbable lightweight mesh is inserted into the extraperitoneal space and secured to Cooper's ligament using absorbable tags. The surgical field shows no visible blood loss.
